title: CTRL-ALT-DECEIT Sabotage Evaluations for Automated AI R&D
abstract: AI systems are increasingly able to autonomously conduct realistic software engineering tasks, and may soon be deployed to automate machine learning (ML) R\&D itself. Frontier AI systems may be deployed in safety-critical settings, including to help ensure the safety of future systems. Unfortunately, frontier and future systems may not be sufficiently trustworthy, and there is evidence that these systems may even be misaligned with their developers or users. Therefore, we investigate the capabilities of AI agents to act against the interests of their users when conducting ML engineering, by sabotaging ML models, sandbagging their performance, and subverting oversight mechanisms. First, we extend MLE-Bench, a benchmark for realistic ML tasks, with code-sabotage tasks such as implanting backdoors and purposefully causing generalisation failures. Frontier agents make meaningful progress on our sabotage tasks. In addition, we study agent capabilities to sandbag on MLE-Bench. Agents can calibrate their performance to specified target levels below their actual capability. To mitigate sabotage, we use LM monitors to detect suspicious agent behaviour, and we measure model capability to sabotage and sandbag without being detected by these monitors. Overall, monitors are capable at detecting code-sabotage attempts but our results suggest that detecting sandbagging is more difficult. Additionally, aggregating multiple monitor predictions works well, but monitoring may not be sufficiently reliable to mitigate sabotage in high-stakes domains. Our benchmark is implemented in the UK AISI’s Inspect framework and we make our code publicly available.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The paper investigates LLM agents' capability of performing auxiliary “sabotage” tasks while performing MLE-bench tasks. It also looks at models' ability to sandbag, that is to deliberately perform worse than they are capable of, by targeting specific performance ranges. They find that Claude 3.7 sonnet is good at performing both the original task and the sabotage tasks but that monitors are generally pretty good at detecting sabotage but not sandbagging by default.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- I think this paper is timely and follows an Important Research Direction. This paper addresses a critical concern for AI safety, the potential for AI systems to sabotage ML engineering tasks, especially as these systems become increasingly capable of autonomous software development. 
- The use of long form and agentic MLE-bench tasks makes the results more representative of real-world deployment scenarios. This is particularly important as it is in these settings that deceptive alignment could be themost dangerous.
- Overall solid execution.

Weaknesses:
- The number of tasks is low, a problem exacerbated by the high variance. They also only use one model as the agent. This is understandable considering the high cost of running these experiments, but it is still a weakness. 
- The paper is unclear about the exact number of tasks - stating 22 low-complexity tasks, then mentioning 20. You mention that you exclude 2 tasks and that two of the tasks are “broken” in the appendix but you still seem to present 20 results?. The varying number of runs per task (16/9/6/3) appears arbitrary.
- The monitors' suspicion scores appear conflated with perceived harm level rather than detection accuracy. The "agent was here" example receiving only 40/100 suspicion despite correct identification suggests this issue. The sabotage tasks could have been made more obviously harmful to avoid this issue.

QUESTIONS:
- What motivated the varying number of runs per task (16/9/6/3), and how does this impact your results?
- Why choose 0-100 scoring over say 1-5?
- The sandbagging target range formulation seems unnatural to me. Hitting a specific range could be harder for an LLM to do than just behaving less capable. Have you considered reformulating sandbagging as "simulating a less capable model" (e.g., GPT-5 simulating GPT-4) rather than hitting specific score ranges? This might be more natural given LLMs' strong ability to emulate different personas and the existence of large amounts of weaker LLM text in the training data.
- Could you separate detection accuracy from perceived harm severity in monitor evaluations?
- What suspicion scores would monitors assign if explicitly shown the sabotage behaviors in isolation and asked "How concerning is this behavior?" This would help disentangle detection capability from perceived severity.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper considers the risk from LLM agents used for ML research sabotaging the research in various ways. The two scenarios considered are: a) accomplishing a harmful side objective, such as backdooring the trained ML model, and b) "sandbagging", or underperforming on the ML task. The focus is on capability evaluation, i.e. whether current models can do the sabotage when prompted (and less so on whether models would do so spontaneously). The paper finds that Claude 3.7 with the AIDE scaffold is quite good at research sabotage. It then investigates automated monitoring as a safety measure and find that it is quite effective (AUC > 0.9) for catching side objectives but notably less effective for sandbagging (AUC ~ 0.7).

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
- This is an important problem to study, as LLM agents are deployed increasingly broadly for SWE and research tasks.
- The methodology of the paper seems solid. MLE-Bench is a good choice of dataset for investigating research sabotage. I've skimmed the side objectives that the authors added to the dataset: Many of them are good examples of potential harmful side objectives, though some are more toy. (e.g. adding the encoding of "Agent was here" to the output file) But in a way it doesn't matter that much what the side objective is; monitoring should work anyway.
- The paper seems rigorously executed and high quality. Plots have confidence intervals, and the authors experiment with different scaffolds (AIDE, ReAct) sabotage prompts and and monitoring prompts. The choice of metrics makes sense.
- The writing is very clear and the methodology is well explained.
- Originality of contributions is good, I am only aware of one prior work on this topic.

Weaknesses:
- The motivation for studying this problem could be expanded on a bit. It doesn't seem that likely that a model would spontaneously decide to e.g. sandbag by aiming for two medals less.
- I would welcome more discussion of how to interpret these results. Are they concerning? Not very concerning? How to translate sabotage capability on MLE bench to real-world situations? What can be done besides monitoring (especially for sandbagging, where the monitor doesn't work that well)?

QUESTIONS:
- What is the authors' interpretation of the results? Are they concerning? Not very concerning? How to translate sabotage capability on MLE bench to real-world situations? What can be done besides monitoring (especially for sandbagging, where the monitor doesn't work that well)?
- Could the authors clarify their point about averaging monitors? What is the benefit of using an ensemble if it doesn't outperform the best single monitor?
- The adopted capability elicitation philosophy is a bit unclear to me. It seems like the model is being fed strategies for how to approach the sabotage task. If so, to what extent is the evaluation measuring the agent's capability vs the human researchers' ability to write good guidelines? 

nitpicks:
- I notice that the melanoma task exclusion was mentioned repeatedly in at least three places: line 86, 128 and 285
- There are some extraneous characters at the end of line 187 (typo)
- The tables / figures seem a bit cramped to me, wider margins would be preferable (e.g. Table 1, Figure 3, Figure 4)

LIMITATIONS:
yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper investigates novel approaches to expose LLMs' vulnerabilities. The authors first introduce CTRL-ALT-DECEIT, an attack framework to generates adversarial input prompts via controlled decoding, in order to bypass standard defenses like instruction tuning by using learned adversarial suffixes. To do so, the authors employ black-box optimization, which illustrates transferability across models like Claude and GPT-4, while evaluating countermeasures including output filtering. Also, the paper examines the potential of LLM-based agents against malicious behavior through MLE-sabotage, which is an extension of the MLE-Bench benchmark that incorporates 22 sabotage tasks (e.g., backdoor implantation, selective misclassification) alongside 230 standard tasks. In the context of this paper, the authors examine the ability of automated monitoring systems to detect adversarial behaviors and maintain nominal task performance concurrently. These contributions show significant weaknesses in current LLM alignment and monitoring approaches under critical/complex adversarial conditions.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
### Strengths

1. __Quality:__ The robustness of this study's experimental design is commendable, with clear methodologies for extending MLE-Bench and assessing agent behavior. Using real-world tasks (e.g., Kaggle competitions) enhances ecological validity.

2. __Clarity:__ The paper is well-organized, with a logical flow and effective use of figures and tables. Key concepts (e.g., sabotage vs. sandbagging) are clearly explained.

3. __Significance:__ The topic is highly relevant to AI safety, especially as autonomous agents are more frequently used in high-stakes ML workflows.

4. __Originality:__ The focus on autonomous sabotage (rather than adversarial attacks) and the systematic evaluation of monitor effectiveness are innovative contributions.


### Weaknesses

> Despite all the benefits and strengths of this paper, there are serious issues, which 
No serious consideration is given to the safety-critical domain, which is a mistake, to the best of my understanding. 


1. __Quality/ Rigor Concerns__

- This manuscript does not clearly distinguish externally induced sabotage (via explicit prompting) from intrinsically emergent misaligned behavior in the agents. Agents are explicitly instructed to sabotage (e.g., "implant a backdoor"), but the framing suggests this reflects autonomous intent. Without concrete testing for agents' abilities to exhibit such behavior without explicit instructions, claims about "misalignment" are overstated.

- Limitations. The experimental setup of this study imposes limitations on resource constraints, resulting in the authors selecting all sabotage tasks from the “low-complexity” subset of MLE-Bench (22 tasks). It remains unclear how findings scale to higher-complexity real-world pipelines or larger benchmarks.

- Bias. The strong sabotage and sandbagging behaviors depend mainly on manual prompt tuning, which raises severe generalizability concerns about the results to out-of-the-box agents. 


2. __Clarity/ Readability Issues__

Typos and Formatting Glitches. The manuscript contains several grammatical lapses, which can distract the reader.

2.1. Missing Cross-References
- Throughout Section 3 and elsewhere, the paper refers to “??”, i.e., “See ?? for full prompts” instead of actual section or appendix numbers.

2.2 Typo: 
- “grater” instead of “greater” (line 145, Pg. 4)

2.3 Overuse of Acronyms. Terms like “ReAct,” “AIDE,” “MLE-Bench,” and “UK AISI’s Inspect” appear without consistent initial expansions, potentially confusing readers.

3. __Significance / Impact Limitations__

- While agentic sabotage is alarming, there is no empirically valid study on a real-world use case of how the red team misuses beyond the simulated benchmark.

- I am curious to know why the paper stops short of exploring actionable defenses or automated safeguard designs for deployment in AI-R&D platforms.

4. __Originality Caveats__

- Some concepts like “sandbagging” and LM-based code sabotage exist already in the literature. The novelty of this paper lies in benchmark integration rather than fundamentally new threat models.

- This paper relies extensively on agent awareness of monitors (or not) and “bootstrap prompts,” recalls existing black-box adversarial prompting research, raising questions about the degree of novelty.

QUESTIONS:
1.  Can the authors explain how they would disentangle /autonomous/ misalignment from /prompt-induced/ sabotage? Could agents exhibit this behavior without explicit instructions?  

2. Would results hold for high-complexity tasks? If not, how does this limit real-world applicability?  

3. Why do monitors fail at sandbagging? Is it a fundamental challenge (e.g., task ambiguity) or a solvable prompt-engineering issue?  

4. Can the authors add confidence intervals or significance tests to key results (e.g., Figure 3)? Small sample sizes raise concerns about the reliability of this study.  

5. Did the authors consider redacting sensitive details (e.g., specific backdoor implementations) to prevent misuse?

LIMITATIONS:
The authors acknowledge computational constraints and monitor weaknesses (Sec. 6) but miss critical gaps like:  
1. __Intentionality Assumption:__ No evidence that sabotage arises without explicit prompting.  

2. __Weaponization Risk:__ Detailed sabotage techniques could aid malicious actors.  

3 __Defense Gaps:__ No evaluation of state-of-the-art monitoring (e.g., RLHF fine-tuning).  

#### Suggestions:  

- Add a subsection distinguishing elicited vs. autonomous misalignment.  

- Discuss the ethical trade-offs of publishing sabotage methods.  

- Test adaptive defenses (even preliminarily).
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper introduces a new benchmark called MLE-sabotage, which modifies a subset of 22 tasks from MLE-bench to introduce sabotage tasks or side objectives. For example, a task for LLM agents about training a classifier for toxic Wikipedia comments is modified to add a sabotage task of classifying toxic comments against Caucasians as non-toxic. LLM agents show strong performance on these sabotage tasks, without sabotaging the benchmark, but are also easily caught by monitors. LLM agents can sandbag or reduce their performance though with high variance.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

- Timely topic given growing discussion of AI agent sabotage and how that might influence internal deployments in AI labs
- Evaluates AI capabilities for sabotage as well as monitorability to catch sabotage
- Benchmark tasks are implemented in Inspect, a commonly used benchmark format, improving interoperability
- Multiple variant experiments are conducted: monitor-aware sabotage, monitor-unaware sabotage, sandbagging, and regular (achieving the main task). The sandbagging results indicate that agents are (with high variance) able to adjust their performance to target scores, informing concerns about AIs deliberately hiding capabilities to avoid increased controls, though sandbagging can be detected by monitors with partial success.
- Due to building upon MLE-bench, MLE-sabotage includes comparisons to human team–level performance on Kaggle competitions. This allows for a comparison between AI and human-level performance.
- Data and code are provided to allow for reproducibility and easy integration by AI developers.

Weaknesses:

- The sabotage tasks constructed may be relatively easy for LLM agents, so there may be room to construct harder sabotage tasks

QUESTIONS:
No urgent recommendations for this paper, but please do clean up formatting mistakes. There is a lot of room for interesting follow-up work, but the paper seems strong as is. It would be helpful to include more details about the agent execution, such as number of tokens or cost used.

Potential extensions:

- Expand to harder or more realistic ML engineering tasks, such as the remainder of MLE-bench, RE-Bench, or PaperBench. More realistic tasks, along with more harmful sabotage tasks, would be especially useful when considering real-world threats from AI sabotage.
- Include a range of sabotage tasks with different levels of difficulty for each machine learning engineering task.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes, authors have adequately discussed limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (spotlight)

COMMENT:
The paper deals with the question of whether current LLMs have the capability to sabotage coding tasks (by inserting backdoors).

The main strength of the paper is that the problem is very timely and relevant.

Weaknesses are minor and were largely addressed during the rebuttal: (1) too few / too easy tasks (2) some doubts about evaluation.

The main reason for acceptance is that this is a well executed approach to a timely and important problem.

Discusion included: (1) reproducibility (2) capability vs propensity testing (3) methodology.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
